ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper combines two established paradigms—annotation projection with heuristic sentence selection and representation projection via linear embedding mappings—and adds co-decoding schemes to merge their outputs. These components individually are well-studied in cross-lingual transfer; the main novelty is the particular heuristic sentence-quality metric and the co-decoding design rather than a principled new formulation. From a theory-focused perspective this reads as an incremental contribution: useful engineering and empirical tuning but lacking principled justification for the heuristics or formal advances over prior projection methods. | B(3): The paper assembles a practical pipeline combining two established ideas—annotation projection with a quality-based sentence selection heuristic and representation projection via linear embedding mapping to apply a single English NER model—and adds heuristic co-decoding to fuse their outputs. Each component (annotation projection, bilingual embedding mapping, and ensemble/co-decoding) is known in prior work, so the contribution is largely an engineering-driven combination rather than a fundamentally new algorithm. That said, the specific data-selection metric and co-decoding recipes are useful, reproducible refinements that would inform practitioners deploying low-resource cross-lingual NER.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper combines two established paradigms—annotation projection with heuristic sentence selection and representation projection via linear embedding mappings—and adds co-decoding schemes to merge their outputs. These components individually are well-studied in cross-lingual transfer; the main novelty is the particular heuristic sentence-quality metric and the co-decoding design rather than a principled new formulation. From a theory-focused perspective this reads as an incremental contribution: useful engineering and empirical tuning but lacking principled justification for the heuristics or formal advances over prior projection methods.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper assembles a practical pipeline combining two established ideas—annotation projection with a quality-based sentence selection heuristic and representation projection via linear embedding mapping to apply a single English NER model—and adds heuristic co-decoding to fuse their outputs. Each component (annotation projection, bilingual embedding mapping, and ensemble/co-decoding) is known in prior work, so the contribution is largely an engineering-driven combination rather than a fundamentally new algorithm. That said, the specific data-selection metric and co-decoding recipes are useful, reproducible refinements that would inform practitioners deploying low-resource cross-lingual NER.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized and readable (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early (annotation projection with sentence-quality selection, representation projection with linear mapping, and co-decoding). Notation and hyperparameter reporting are adequate and figures/tables (datasets, baselines, F1/precision/recall results) appear informative, supporting reproducibility. I deduct one point because the manuscript omits an explicit discussion of limitations and robustness (e.g., assumptions behind the heuristic selection metric and linear mapping), which reduces conceptual clarity for a theory-focused reader. | B(4): The paper appears well organized (intro → methods → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states the main contributions early (annotation projection, representation projection, co-decoding). Notation and hyperparameter reporting are adequate and writing quality is good, with related work coverage described as sufficient. Minor presentation gaps include no stated limitations and no mention of code or whether figures/tables effectively illustrate results, which prevents a perfect score from a practitioner’s reproducibility standpoint.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized and readable (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early (annotation projection with sentence-quality selection, representation projection with linear mapping, and co-decoding). Notation and hyperparameter reporting are adequate and figures/tables (datasets, baselines, F1/precision/recall results) appear informative, supporting reproducibility. I deduct one point because the manuscript omits an explicit discussion of limitations and robustness (e.g., assumptions behind the heuristic selection metric and linear mapping), which reduces conceptual clarity for a theory-focused reader.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper appears well organized (intro → methods → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states the main contributions early (annotation projection, representation projection, co-decoding). Notation and hyperparameter reporting are adequate and writing quality is good, with related work coverage described as sufficient. Minor presentation gaps include no stated limitations and no mention of code or whether figures/tables effectively illustrate results, which prevents a perfect score from a practitioner’s reproducibility standpoint.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a meaningful practical contribution by combining annotation and representation projection with co-decoding to improve cross-lingual NER, and that the methods are likely to be useful to practitioners. I give a 4 because the empirical gains and pragmatic approach indicate strong applied impact, but remaining concerns about heuristic choices, theoretical justification, robustness checks, and limited reproducibility prevent scoring it as fully transformative.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—weakly supervised cross-lingual NER—and demonstrates a useful empirical advance by combining annotation projection and representation projection with co-decoding to outperform prior methods on CoNLL data. However, from a theory-oriented perspective the work relies on heuristic sentence-quality selection and linear mapping assumptions without principled justification or thorough ablation/robustness analyses across seeds and languages; no limitations are stated and code availability is unclear. These omissions limit the influence on how researchers will reconceptualize cross-lingual transfer, though the approach is a solid, practically useful contribution that can catalyze follow-up work if stronger diagnostics and theoretical grounding are added.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important practical problem—cross-lingual NER with minimal human annotation—using simple, resource-efficient techniques (heuristic selection for annotation projection and linear embedding mapping) that produced empirical gains and improved over prior cross-lingual baselines on CoNLL test sets. The combination via co-decoding is a pragmatic engineering contribution likely to be adopted by practitioners seeking low-resource transfer. Reproducibility is somewhat limited by use of in-house annotated sets and no mention of released code, so while the impact is strong and broadly applicable, it stops short of being fully transformative.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper uses established CoNLL benchmarks and standard metrics and compares to prior cross-lingual NER work, which supports largely fair evaluation. However, they also concur that the baseline set is narrow (only two prior systems, one older), some newer competing approaches are omitted, and there is insufficient clarity about identical evaluation conditions and statistical/significance or ablation analyses. Given these strengths but notable omissions and clarity gaps, a middle score reflecting limited but not inadequate comparison is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against two prior works (Täckström et al. 2012; Tsai et al. 2016) and evaluates on standard CoNLL test sets, so it does include established baselines and common metrics. However, the summary suggests limited breadth — only two baselines (one quite old) and no mention of more recent cross-lingual or transfer methods, nor clear statements that ensure identical evaluation conditions across methods. From a theory-focused perspective I also note no indication that the authors report statistical significance or ablations isolating their heuristic selection and mapping components when comparing to prior work, so the comparisons are incomplete and potentially unfair.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares to established cross-lingual NER baselines (Täckström et al. 2012 and Tsai et al. 2016) and evaluates on standard CoNLL datasets using F1/precision/recall, which supports largely fair comparisons. However, only two prior systems are included (missing some more recent embedding- or multilingual-model baselines), and use of in-house annotated data for some languages leaves a few questions about exact parity of conditions. Overall the comparisons are solid and reproducible on CoNLL but have minor omissions of newer competing approaches and some clarity gaps about identical training/evaluation setups.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper clearly states its claims and reports multi-language empirical gains, but they also note important methodological shortcomings: heuristic components lack principled justification and ablations, there is no statistical testing or robustness checks, and reproducibility is limited by unspecified in-house data and no code release. These weaknesses reduce confidence in the correctness and generality of the findings, so a mid-level score is appropriate until the authors provide stronger validation and transparency.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper makes clear claims and presents multi-language experimental results showing gains over prior work, which supports the main empirical claim. However, from the summary there is no principled/theoretical justification for the frequency-based sentence-quality metric or the heuristic selection, and the paper appears to lack thorough ablations and diagnostics validating key components (e.g., quality metric, linearity assumption for embedding mapping). Experimental methodology is reasonable in using CoNLL datasets and prior baselines, but I see no mention of statistical significance testing, multiple runs/seeds, or robustness checks—weakening confidence in the claimed improvements. Overall the contribution is promising but some core steps are heuristically justified and under-validated, so I deduct points for missing analyses and unstated assumptions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and provides empirical evidence on CoNLL benchmarks and in-house annotated languages showing that annotation projection, representation projection, and co-decoding improve cross-lingual NER compared to Täckström et al. (2012) and Tsai et al. (2016). There are no complex mathematical derivations to verify and the described linear embedding mapping approach is standard and appears correctly applied. My concerns are methodological: reliance on unspecified in-house annotated data, no mention of statistical significance testing, and no code release, which limits reproducibility and assessment of robustness. Overall the conclusions are plausible and supported by the reported experiments, but minor issues around reproducibility and statistical validation prevent a top score.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important, timely cross-lingual NER problem with appropriate datasets, baselines, and evaluations, making it a good fit for a top-tier NLP venue. However, Reviewer A’s concerns about heuristic components, reliance on in-house annotations, and limited robustness/ablation analyses suggest the contribution is somewhat less polished or generalizable than ideal, so a near-top score (4) best reflects strong relevance but some methodological/reproducibility shortcomings that should be addressed.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a central, timely NLP problem (cross-lingual NER) using methods and evaluation (CoNLL datasets, F1/precision/recall, comparisons to Täckström et al. and Tsai et al.) that are squarely within top-tier conference scope. However, from a theory-focused perspective the work relies on heuristic components (frequency-based sentence-quality metric, ad-hoc selection and co-decoding) and uses several in-house annotated corpora without stated limitations or robustness analyses, which raises concerns about generality and reproducibility. These issues do not remove relevance to the venue but warrant stronger diagnostics/ablation or theoretical justification to reach a clear ‘‘perfect fit.’’

reviewer_b.justification: This paper fits squarely within top-tier NLP venues: it tackles cross-lingual NER with practical, reproducible-seeming methods (annotation projection + embedding mapping + co-decoding) and shows empirical gains on standard CoNLL benchmarks. The problem formulation, neural architectures, and evaluation (precision/recall/F1 against established baselines) match community expectations, and the emphasis on simple, resource-efficient transfer techniques addresses the applied multilingual audience I represent. While code availability isn’t mentioned, the method and baselines are clear and directly relevant to researchers and practitioners in the field.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical evaluation is broad and compares to reasonable baselines across multiple languages, and that hyperparameters and several model variants are reported. However, they also agree the paper relies on heuristic components without adequate ablation/sensitivity analyses, statistical robustness checks, or discussion of limitations and code release; these omissions meaningfully weaken the methodological rigor. Given the solid empirical scope but the notable gaps in validation and analysis, a mid-tier score of 3 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: Experiments cover multiple languages (CoNLL datasets plus in-house annotations) and compare to reasonable baselines, supporting the core empirical claims; hyperparameters are reported and two model architectures and co-decoding are evaluated. However, the paper introduces heuristic components (frequency-based sentence-quality metric, heuristic selection, co-decoding rules) without reported formal justification or thorough ablation/sensitivity analyses validating each component independently, and no statistical significance or robustness checks across seeds are mentioned. The related work appears adequate, but the absence of discussed limitations, failure cases, code release, and theoretical grounding for the core projection/selection choices prevents a higher score.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are fairly comprehensive: they evaluate on multiple CoNLL languages and additional in-house languages, compare to reasonable prior baselines, and report P/R/F1 and hyperparameters. The paper proposes and evaluates multiple components (annotation projection with selection, representation projection via linear mapping, two NN variants, and co-decoding) but the summary does not mention explicit ablation or sensitivity analyses isolating each component’s contribution or robustness to selection thresholds. Related work is covered adequately, and practical reproduction is aided by hyperparameter reporting, but code/models aren’t mentioned and limitations/failure cases are not discussed, so the empirical evidence is strong but missing some critical analyses for full rigor.

main_claims: Introduce annotation projection with a heuristic data selection scheme to create weakly labeled NER data in a target language without human annotation.
Propose a representation projection approach that maps target-language word embeddings into English and applies a single English NER system (NN1/NN2) for direct cross-lingual transfer.

Design two co-decoding schemes to combine annotation-projection and representation-projection outputs, and show the combined system outperforms previous cross-lingual NER approaches on CoNLL test data.
_summary.methodology: Train an English NER neural model that uses only word embeddings (two architectures NN1 and NN2); (1) create weakly labeled target data via annotation projection with a frequency-based sentence-quality metric and heuristic selection; (2) train monolingual embeddings and learn linear mappings to project target embeddings into English and decode with the English NER model; finally combine both approaches with co-decoding.
datasets: In-house human-annotated NER data for Japanese
In-house human-annotated NER data for Korean
In-house human-annotated NER data for German
In-house human-annotated NER data for Portuguese

reviewer_a.name: Theory-Focused Purist
reviewer_a.background: Senior NLP researcher with a PhD in computational linguistics and a history of publishing formal analyses of learning algorithms and representational methods.
traits: Penalizes papers that rely on heuristic procedures without formal or principled justification (e.g., ad-hoc selection metrics) — deducts points when core steps lack theoretical grounding.

Requires thorough ablation and diagnostic analyses for each proposed component — deducts points if key components (e.g., sentence-quality metric, linear mapping assumptions) are not individually validated.
Demands statistical significance testing and robustness checks across multiple seeds/languages — penalizes single-run results or incomplete variance reporting.
Penalizes lack of clear assumptions and limitations discussion, especially when proposing cross-lingual transfer methods that may fail under common real-world conditions.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I evaluate primarily on conceptual clarity, principled justification, and rigorous analysis: empirical gains must be accompanied by theory or comprehensive diagnostics explaining why and when the method works.

reviewer_b.name: Practical Multilingual Engineer
reviewer_b.background: Applied NLP researcher with experience deploying cross-lingual and low-resource systems in industry and several applied publications on practical transfer methods.
traits: Rewards clear empirical improvements on established benchmarks (e.g., CoNLL) and practical utility — gives points for reproducible gains and simplicity that aid real-world adoption.
Favors simple, resource-efficient methods (e.g., linear mapping of embeddings, heuristic selection) that work across many languages — rewards work that lowers annotation needs.
Values method combinations and engineering (e.g., co-decoding) that demonstrably improve end metrics — gives credit for practical ensemble strategies even if heuristically motivated.
Rewards availability of code, trained models, or clear recipes for replicating results; penalizes papers that provide no practical guidance for reuse.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I prioritize practicality and reproducible empirical gains: methods that are simple, broadly applicable, and improve benchmark performance are valuable even if some components are heuristic.
